Flow fails with the error message .flowconfig:326 Unable to parse line.

### Workaround
Currently, there is no known workaround for this issue.

### Additional Information

- Yarn version: 1.22.19
- Node.js version: v20.9.0
- Windows version : 11 23H2

body:

React version: 18.2.0

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Define count 0 in useState. 
2. Create a useInterval hook.
3. Link useInterval with a callback function that changes the count to 1 in the component.

5. The log should be 0, 1, but the log is 0, 1, 1. In other words, when the same value is entered into setState, rerendering occurs once. (state is a raw value)

Re-rendering should not occur when you enter the same value as before in setState.
author: yoonminsang

title: Bug: Components rendered in React.Suspense can't have their size reliably measured
body:
Components that are rendered in a React.Suspense, along lazy loaded components don't get their layouts measured correctly. This happens because the measurement is done while the lazy loaded components are still loading, so the fallback is being shown. 
After the lazy loaded components are loaded and everything is finally painted to the DOM, useEffects/useLayoutEffects/etc are never retriggered, so measurements can never be taken of the components. 

A way to circumvent this is to use ResizeObserver, but as by react documentation useEffect should be triggered after painting. However that is wrong in the case of React.Suspense/React.lazy.

React version: 18.2.0

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Define a component that does layout measurement using a ref/state/whatever
2. Create a React.Suspense
3. Lazy load a few times that component, and make it take a while to render (500/1000 ms). This can be done with a timeout.
4. The component that takes the longest to render will get correct layout measurements. The rest will be measured incorrectly (have a measurement of 0, since they are measured before they are painted)
  1. If all components take the same time to load, they will all get wrong measurements

Link to code example:
https://codesandbox.io/s/react-typescript-forked-qbhfwb?file=/src/App.tsx

## The current behavior
Measurements are calculated as 0 in useEffect since it triggers before painting, and they are never recomputed in any useEffect/useLayoutEffect after painting to DOM happens.

## The expected behavior
useEffect/useLayoutEffect should instead trigger after the DOM has been updated and the React.Suspense has successfully resolved.

body:
I don't know where else to ask for this but I need to ask: Please, keep the React Class Components paradigm. I found it is a very easy way to do React programming. I understand your team wants to have a better way to develop React components through React Functions but the React Class Components strategy is top notch even if it is as today deprecated. It is said that with React Class Components everything that can be done with React Functions can be done with React Components but the inverse is not true, yet and this proves the power behind React Class Components. A few "exceptions" can lead to infinite possibilities.
        Therefore, I believe, as many others, that the use of React Class components is truly a necessity that seeks to use a wonderful paradigm.

Thanks

title: Bug: useSyncExternalStore does not schedule update after mutation
body:
Here is a Replay recording with comments:
https://app.replay.io/recording/318114a4-3102-4732-ad1b-cb63b9c0ac22

I think the comments I've added show the following scenario:
1. A React component (`Subscriber`) uses `useSyncExternalStore` to read from a mutable store.
2. The Activity subtree containing `Subscriber` is hidden, and React unsubscribes from the store.
3. An update is scheduled to show the hidden subtree again and the `Subscriber` component re-renders (with memoized state).
4. A parent component mutates the store in a layout effect. (The `Subscriber` component is not yet listening and so it does not re-render to reflect the updated store value.)
6. React re-subscribes the `Subscriber` component (`useSyncExternalStore`) but it has already missed the mutation **and React does not check for a changed snapshot value**.

We originally observed this behavior in Replay itself but I was able to reduce it to the following simplified case:
https://codesandbox.io/s/inspiring-bird-m4wv5l

I've added comments to the Replay, including ones that bracket the problematic `commitRoot`. Here's a short Loom as well talking through the bug:
https://www.loom.com/share/2584cad5b4c44e6bba396ff8cf79db1d

I _think_ an application could work around this issue in a couple of ways:
* Only mutate stores in passive effects. (This may cause visible layout shift though so it seems bad.)
* Mutable store users should avoid memoized `getSnapshot` and `subscribe` functions. (This would schedule some unnecessary effects work which is probably nice to avoid but maybe acceptable.)
* **Store subscribe methods should always invoke the `callback` React passes.** (This would cause a lot of unnecessary `Object.is` comparisons but that's probably an acceptable cost.)

I think the third option above seems best, but needing to do that feels like a foot gun for the API. Thoughts?

cc @acdlite, @sebmarkbage in case you find this interesting.
author: bvaughn

title: Feature Request: Support the `defaultSelected` Property for Rendered `<option>` Elements
body:
## The Problem

The browser's _only_ reliable way of identifying default values for `select` elements is the `selected` attribute (which controls the `defaultSelected` property). And default values are very important. Particularly, they're important if you need the ability to 1) [`Reset`](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/HTMLFormElement/reset) a form's values or 2) Track the dirty/clean state of form fields (a very common use case in forms).

Using native browser features, tracking dirty fields is possible with [pure HTML+JS](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/play?id=xDHGGzL2Rl7Kz%2BvqDgEZmBYmzkpvrCytVJCCFYuoRYXdCoUHjzNm4Wu3jh02nHbEBmKCzvbyYf%2BNiBDq) and with other frameworks like [Solid.js](https://playground.solidjs.com/anonymous/b3e76fc8-a00a-4847-98aa-0ff267c624da) and [Vue](https://play.vuejs.org/#). However, [React](https://codesandbox.io/s/react-form-default-values-bug-issue-24067-cqz7c9?file=/src/App.tsx) does not support this feature because it does not support the `selected` attribute on `<option>` elements. What this means is that developers relying on the browser's native features with pure HTML+JS (or with another JS framework) will have a broken application if they migrate to React. It would be _fantastic_ if React could be updated to support the `selected` attribute for important use cases like these.

## The Solution
Support the `selected` attribute for the `option` element. Alternatively, create a new `defaultSelected` attribute (to keep in step with `defaultValue`, `defaultChecked`, etc.) that supports this use case.

Note that the documentation surrounding the `<select>` element would need to be updated if this change is made.

## Motivation
In addition to what I mentioned in the `Problem Statement`, it's worth considering that with the dawning of [Remix](https://remix.run/) and the close pursuit of [SvelteKit](https://kit.svelte.dev/) and [SolidStart](https://start.solidjs.com/), many web developers are returning to the benefits of using the browser's native features for forms to support progressive enhancement and to simplify their codebases. Even the React docs have acknowledged this by the fact that they no longer discourage uncontrolled inputs. That being the case, I feel like supporting this feature is a must. If for some reason `selected` is reserved, then `defaultSelected` could be used instead. What's most important is having a way to support the browser's "default value" features for _all_ native form controls.

title: Bug: useReducer triggers reducer one more time in React 18 Responsive Mode
body:
<!--
  Please provide a clear and concise description of what the bug is. Include
  screenshots if needed. Please test using the latest version of the relevant
  React packages to make sure your issue has not already been fixed.
-->

React version: 18.x.x

## Steps To Reproduce

```ts
import { useReducer } from "react";

function reducer(state, action) {
  console.log(action.type);
}

const initialState = { name: "Taylor", age: 42 };

export default function Form() {
  const [state, dispatch] = useReducer(reducer, initialState);

  function handleClick() {
    dispatch({ type: "click" });
  }

  function handleMouseMove() {
    dispatch({ type: "move" });
  }

  return (
    <button onClick={handleClick} onMouseMove={handleMouseMove}>
      Click Me in Responsive Mode
    </button>
  );
}
```

1. Run the code above. It has a button with handlers for click and mouse move, each triggering a dispatch.
2. Switch to Responsive mode via dev tools, so you can use Touch event.
3. Press the button.
4. You will get in console: click, move, click.
5. In React <=17, the order would have been: move, click. The first click would not have happened, which actually is what I would expect.

Repro Sandbox: https://codesandbox.io/s/rough-water-w3fh5k?file=/App.js

This extra click seems to happen only when the element also has an onMouseMove handler attached to it, and which in turn triggers a dispatch.

Our problem in `downshift` comes from the fact that we are adding both click and mouse move handlers to an element, both which can trigger state updates. This issue impacts our users that want to migrate to React18 and expose their Selects / Comboboxes for mobiles.

title: Bug: New Suspense boundaries not shown until existing Suspense boundaries have resolved during transition
body:
From [this thread](https://github.com/reactwg/react-18/discussions/94#discussioncomment-1406166): > `startTransition` only avoids retriggering Suspense boundaries, new Suspense boundaries will still be shown However, it seems that new `Suspense` boundaries are not shown until existing `Suspense` boundaries have fully resolved (the new content is displayed), which can lead to a jarring user experience, for example in the [React Server Components demo](https://github.com/reactjs/server-components-demo) when delays are introduced to the server components. React version: 18.3.0-next-1308e49a6-20230330 ## Steps To Reproduce 1. Clone the [React Server Components demo](https://github.com/reactjs/server-components-demo) 2. Add a delay of 1 second to the NoteList component and a delay of 3 seconds to the Note component by uncommenting the `/sleep` api calls and changing their delay values. To help understanding this ticket, here are the key pieces of the main `App` component: ```jsx export default function App({selectedId, isEditing, searchText}) { return (
{/* ... */}
}>
}>
); } ``` When clicking on a note item in the sidebar (NoteList component), the `selectedId` is updated and `startTransition` is used to wrap the route change. ## The current behavior - The `Suspense` wrapping `NoteList` does not retrigger, as expected since the route change is wrapped in `startTransition` ([documentation reference](https://react.dev/reference/react/useTransition#preventing-unwanted-loading-indicators)). - The `Suspense` wrapping `Note` **does not retrigger for 1 second**, then finally retriggers and the `NoteSkeleton` is shown for the remaining 2 seconds of delay. It is expected to retrigger even though `startTransition` is used because of the changed `key` on the parent `
` tag ([documentation reference](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense#resetting-suspense-boundaries-on-navigation)). ## The expected behavior - The `Suspense` wrapping `NoteList` does not retrigger, as expected since the route change is wrapped in `startTransition`. - The `Suspense` wrapping `Note` **immediately** retriggers and the `NoteSkeleton` is shown for **all 3 seconds** of delay.

title: Bug: pending attribute from useFormStatus is stuck as "true" when form action takes longer than ~5s to resolve
body:
<!--
  Please provide a clear and concise description of what the bug is. Include
  screenshots if needed. Please test using the latest version of the relevant
  React packages to make sure your issue has not already been fixed.
-->

React version: canary

## Steps To Reproduce

1. open example link-This is a demo example from the official website, only the settimeout time has been changed.（form action resolve promise after 10 seconds）
2. click submit button
3. see the button text

<!--
  Your bug will get fixed much faster if we can run your code and it doesn't
  have dependencies other than React. Issues without reproduction steps or
  code examples may be immediately closed as not actionable.
-->

Link to code example: https://codesandbox.io/s/useformstatus-vzn8tm?file=/App.js:129-142

<!--
  Please provide a CodeSandbox (https://codesandbox.io/s/new), a link to a
  repository on GitHub, or provide a minimal code example that reproduces the
  problem. You may provide a screenshot of the application if you think it is
  relevant to your bug report. Here are some tips for providing a minimal
  example: https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve.
-->

## The current behavior
The button triggers the form action, and the pending useFormStatus is always true, even if the action is completed.



## The expected behavior
The button triggers the form action. After the action is executed, useFormStatus should change from pending-true to pending-false.


Debug found that when the code is executed here-`renderRootSync`, `throwValue` has actually been resolved. If `throwAndUnwindWorkLoop` adds a then callback after resolved, it will not be triggered.
![image](https://github.com/facebook/react/assets/15187953/d356b9d8-ee1a-47ab-b131-031c46faa24c)


If you change the settimeout timer in the form action to a smaller value, such as 1000ms, then the code will go here `renderRootConcurrent`, and `replaySuspendedUnitOfWork` can start to re-render.
![image](https://github.com/facebook/react/assets/15187953/579a05d0-f926-463c-9ed2-2027816f5194)

body:
<!--
  Please provide a clear and concise description of what the bug is. Include
  screenshots if needed. Please test using the latest version of the relevant
  React packages to make sure your issue has not already been fixed.
-->

React version: 18.2.0

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Set up multiple nested suspense boundaries in the manner done in the below codesandbox
2. When promise resolves, React fails to clear one of the suspense boundaries because it can't find the hidden div on the DOM
that it's expecting

<!--
  Your bug will get fixed much faster if we can run your code and it doesn't
  have dependencies other than React. Issues without reproduction steps or
  code examples may be immediately closed as not actionable.
-->

Link to code example: https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/thirsty-monad-tgccvt

<!--
  Please provide a CodeSandbox (https://codesandbox.io/s/new), a link to a
  repository on GitHub, or provide a minimal code example that reproduces the
  problem. You may provide a screenshot of the application if you think it is
  relevant to your bug report. Here are some tips for providing a minimal
  example: https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve.
-->

## The current behavior
It's hard to discern exactly what is triggering the issue here, but basically I have a setup where a server component renders a client component that has a suspense boundary inside it, triggered by its own child which uses `use` to wait on the passed in promise. It also renders any provided children, both in the "fallback" state of its suspense boundary and in the normal case. The children are being passed in as another server component tree that has its own suspense boundary, which is also waiting on the same promise.

When the promise is resolved, the DOM contents are updated as expected but there seems to be an internal React error complaining that one of the `$RC` calls its embedding on the page can't find the element its expecting in order to "removeChild". I think that resolving the first suspense boundary is causing the second one to be removed unexpectedly or something, and React is throwing an error because it can't be found.


## The expected behavior
Either this should resolve correctly, or if I'm doing something that can't be supported properly I would hope for a descriptive error message.

title: Bug: manipulating controlled input value breaks native validation
body:
<!--
  Please provide a clear and concise description of what the bug is. Include
  screenshots if needed. Please test using the latest version of the relevant
  React packages to make sure your issue has not already been fixed.
-->
If you have a controlled input with some restrictions(like minLength for example) manipulating input value in any way in onChange handler breaks native validation.
For example if you have ` onChange={e => setText(e.target.value.toUpperCase())}}` this will break if your value after applying uppercase is different than the initial target value. So an input like "123" works but "a" doesn't - in the latter example HTML will consider input value to be valid.

React version:
18.0.0

## Steps To Reproduce
You can check-out code example below which showcases the issue - if you input any letter the background will turn green immediately indicating the the value is valid even though it doesn't meet minLengh requirement. If you input a number instead it works as intended(because `"1"` is the same as `"1".toUpperCase()`)

Link to code example:
https://playcode.io/1645770


## The current behavior
Explained above

## The expected behavior
It should handle validation correctly as with regular input

title: Bug: flushSync on component having useLayoutEffect in some cases causes Maximum update depth exceeded
body:
React version: 18.2.0

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Click button `Click me` in the code example.
2. You can see in console that the component gets re-rendered about 50 times and then React stops it.

Link to code example: https://codesandbox.io/s/infallible-joana-ysrxxt?file=/src/App.js
![screenshot-after-click-me](https://github.com/facebook/react/assets/4648469/427c875e-cb4f-42c7-8d66-a572688c79c0)


## The current behavior
It fails with `Maximum update depth exceeded` message.

## The expected behavior
It should stabilize after few (2-4) re-renders, it definitely should not fail.

What seems to be happenning:
1. After clicking the button, code waits for 500ms and then a state update for `complexState` is scheduled (but not yet processed) on row marked as R2.
2. flushSync is called with another state update (R3). Seems like this update is performed by applying it to the last committed state, i.e. ignoring the state update that is scheduled by R2.
3. After the commit of R3, layout effect depending on the `complexState` is performed, leading to state update, but of different state hook - `counter` state.
4. At this point, I would expect that the component simply re-renders with the new `counter` state and does not trigger the layout effect (effect depending on `complexState`). Alas, it seems to re-run the state-updater from R3 (again!), which then leads to triggerring the layout effect again and hence the loop.
5. Just for completeness - the step 4 would conclude the flushSync part of the example, and the component would then probably re-render again ~2 times, now applying both R2+R3 updaters (because R2 seems to be still only scheduled, not processed) and then `counter` update in layout effect would follow.

What I think is wrong is the re-applying of R3 state-updater in step 4 after layout effect (I think this state update should be considered as already committed). I don't know the React codebase much so the issue might be something else, e.g. maybe the `counter` state update in the layout effect gets applied to wrong committed state (as if it started off from the state that was there before user clicked the button, and React tries to apply accumulated changes, i.e. R3 + `counter` update?).

Other notes:
- If you comment out R2, or replace useLayoutEffect by useEffect, all works as expected (but that should not be considered as a fix because you need useLayoutEffect to force an effect before paint, and you need flushSync for the same outside of effect, e.g. in ResizeObserver callback, see https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/24331 ).
- The example is simplified to better showcase the issue. In reality we had several async operations which got resolved and then got processed within the same event loop spin (microtasks), i.e. React scheduled an update with several state updates, but one of those ops had intentionally flushSync (without knowing about the other async ops) and it lead to this failure.

title: Bug: hoisted stylesheets should not reorder when re-rendered in a transition
body:
vercel.com observed a bug where a stylesheet hoisted using a `precedence` was reordered after an update. This is not correct semantics (once a hoisted stylesheet is inserted it should remain in place until removed)

Investigating the React implementation I observed that if you insert a hoisted stylesheet during a synchronous render and later render a new reference to that stylesheet in a transition React incorrectly considers the stylesheet not loaded and tries to coordinate it's loading the the commit. This is erroneous as once a stylesheet is in the DOM it should never be re-inserted (moved).

title: Bug: forwardRef ignore Type Check
body:
React version: 18.2.0 TypeScript version: 5.2.2 ## Steps To Reproduce 1. Define a component with `forwardRef` 2. Do not give type annotation to parameter `ref` 3. Use `useImperativeHandle` 4. Use the component and pass a ref object 5. Access the ref object's properties that are not exposed in `useImperativeHandle` The reproducible code forked from [here](https://react.dev/learn/manipulating-the-dom-with-refs#exposing-a-subset-of-the-api-with-an-imperative-handle): ```typescript 'use client' import { forwardRef, useImperativeHandle, useRef } from "react"; const Child = forwardRef((props, ref) => { const realInputRef = useRef(null) useImperativeHandle(ref, () => ({ // focus() { // realInputRef.current?.focus() // }, })) return }) const Parent = () => { const inputRef = useRef(null) function handleClick() { inputRef.current?.focus() } return (
) } Child.displayName = 'Child' export default Parent ``` Link to code example: https://codesandbox.io/p/sandbox/sweet-cloud-hn34vp ## The current behavior `forwardRef` with `ForwardedRef` does not cause any type error. Then an error occurs like below on run-time. ``` TypeError: _inputRef_current.focus is not a function ``` ## The expected behavior `forwardRef` components should cause an error when wrong type ref objects are passed through the parameter `ref`. It is so that we notice a mistake before run-time.

title: Bug: Nested forms created by createPortal submit the outer form.
body:
When we use react's createPortal, it is supposed to create the element inside the specified element. It works that way, but onSubmit on forms works based on a hierarchy of React components. If we have a parent form and inside it, a Modal renders the form with createPortal connected to `document.body`,  when we fire submit, it also fires the submit of the parent form, which shouldn't be fired.

3.  Try to submit inside the nested form

Link to code example:

## The current behavior
The parent form onSubmit fires.

## The expected behavior
It shouldn't fire.

title: Bug: react-hooks/exhaustive-deps reports a dependency if `typeof` TypeScript operator is used in a function.
body:
These issues linked below are closely related, but I believe the problem was only fixed if you use `typeof` TypeScript operator inside the `useEffect` callback. https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/19327 https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/18828 However, if you call a function from the `useEffect` callback and that function uses `typeof` in a type context, exhaustive-deps reports a problem. React version: 18.2.0 "eslint-plugin-react-hooks": "^4.6.0" ## Steps To Reproduce 1. Set up a project with eslint, eslint-plugin-react-hooks installed. 2. In a useEffect, call a function that uses `typeof` to define the type of a parameter. 3. Run lint with exhaustive-deps rule on. Link to code example: I hope this sample is enough, let me know if it would be useful to have a whole cloneable repository for reproduction. ``` export default function App() { const myNumber = Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000); function printNumber(n: typeof myNumber) { console.log(n); } useEffect(() => { const foo = 3; printNumber(foo); }, []); return

Your random render number: {myNumber}

; } ``` ## The current behavior The above code reports the following problem. ``` React Hook useEffect has a missing dependency: 'printNumber'. Either include it or remove the dependency array react-hooks/exhaustive-deps ``` ## The expected behavior `typeof` operator in the type context does not affect the compiled JavaScript and should not be reported by `exhaustive-deps`. For reference, the following does not report an error even though it's functionally the exact same as the code example above: ``` export default function App() { const myNumber = Math.floor(Math.random() * 1000); function printNumber(n: number) { console.log(n); } useEffect(() => { const foo = 3; printNumber(foo); }, []); return

Your random render number: {myNumber}

; } ```

title: Bug: ReadableStream can be written to after close
body:
As reported in https://github.com/vercel/next.js/issues/56919 it is possible for the ReadableStream controller to be written to after it is already closed. This is because inside an async context a Float method such as `ReactDOM.preload()` can attempt to flush newly created Resources even after the stream has completed.

React version: 

The underlying bug has been in React since Float landed. The expression of the Bug as reported in the Next.js repo was landed in: https://github.com/facebook/react/commit/701ac2e57290f913502c6012c15e815f412f84b7
The first Canary release to have this new expression was: [18.3.0-canary-f81c0f1ed-20230927](https://www.npmjs.com/package/react-dom/v/18.3.0-canary-f81c0f1ed-20230927)


## Steps To Reproduce

1. use `react-dom/server.edge`
2. Ensure the environment supports AsyncLocalStorage
3. Call Float method like `ReactDOM.preload(...)` after an await after the stream has closed

## The current behavior
If the conditions are right the Float call will initiate a write after the stream has closed and there will be a thrown exception

## The expected behavior
The Float call is ignored after the stream has closed. There are no errors

title: Bug: performance deteriorates when using ReactDOM.createRoot instead of ReactDom.render for virtual-table.
body:
While experimenting with react-window, I encountered a performance disparity in my project compared to the smooth user experience demonstrated in the react-window documentation examples. Even after building for production, I noticed white flashes when scrolling quickly in my project, whereas the documentation's examples remained consistently smooth.

After conducting several experiments, I successfully identified the root cause: the performance significantly deteriorates when using ReactDOM.createRoot compared to the now deprecated ReactDOM.render.

Here is a video to illustrate the issue:
https://github.com/facebook/react/assets/7544804/0be9ba6f-47e8-4d61-bb6c-e8fef0787776

This issue is not exclusive to react-window; in fact, I first encountered it while developing a toy version of it using function components and hooks (react-window itself is currently implemented using class components).

React version:
Tried with react/react-dom 18.2.0 and react/react-dom 18.3.0-canary-09fbee89d-20231013

Link to code example:
Here are two CodeSandbox examples of the same scenario with only one distinction:
- The first one uses ReactDOM.render: https://codesandbox.io/s/modest-cdn-4jwd6y?file=/index.js 
- The second one uses ReactDOM.createRoot: https://codesandbox.io/s/magical-cdn-ggyytv?file=/index.js 
**It's worth noting that the issue seems to become more pronounced after building the React app for production.** I'm not sure if that's possible directly on codesandbox.

Additionally, I've created two CodeSandbox using my own simplified version of a virtual table, the performance issue is also noticeable in this scenario:
- Using ReactDom.render: https://codesandbox.io/s/sleepy-bardeen-43dd9g?file=/index.js
- Using ReactDom.createRoot: https://codesandbox.io/s/friendly-wilson-qxs4yc?file=/index.js

## The current behavior
The performance appears to be worse when using ReactDom.createRoot compared to ReactDom.render

## The expected behavior
Using ReactDom.createRoot should give the same performance or better performance than ReactDom.render

title: Bug: Events in separate roots are not batched
body:
React version: 18.2.0

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Render a Parent react component, which uses the ReactDOM apis to create a nested Child react root after mounting
2. In parent, add an onClick handler which updates state on an element which wraps Child
3. In child, add another onClick handler which also updates state.

Note that clicks in the child should trigger both event handlers due to bubbling. 

Link to code example:
https://codesandbox.io/s/kind-snowflake-cw6m9s?file=/src/App.jsx

The text elements have onClick handlers which cause the App component to rerender. Calls to the render() function are logged to the console, so that we can count renders. A single click should only ever cause one rerender due to batching. 

## The current behavior
Clicking on `This is the outer root` or `This is a child in the outer root` results in a single render. Clicking on `This is the child root` renders twice.

## The expected behavior
State updates should be batched despite being in separate React roots. `This is the child root` should also only render once. This is the behavior in React 16: https://codesandbox.io/s/lingering-snow-z83m39?file=/src/index.js

I believe this is due to the event delegation changes in R17. Prior to R17, React only attached one listener to the document body, whereas now we have one listener for each root. Render batching is performed *within* the listener: https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/4337c1c00609ec8d7ae399c736e9d37bb159fac5/packages/react-dom/src/events/DOMPluginEventSystem.js#L632

which prevents event handlers from separate listeners (i.e, roots) from being batched. It's hard to tell if batching is really the expected behavior, but it did happen in React 16 and I found this ancient issue mentioning it: https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/1227

title: Bug: Error Boundary not updating and not calling componentDidCatch despite catching error
body:
<!--
  Please provide a clear and concise description of what the bug is. Include
  screenshots if needed. Please test using the latest version of the relevant
  React packages to make sure your issue has not already been fixed.
-->

React version: 18.2.0

(React 17.0.2 doesn't have this problem)

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Develop a component that throws an error during rendering, **but only once**.
2. Implement an Error Boundary component with a `getDerivedStateFromError` static method, which updates its state to indicate the presence of an error.
3. Enhance the Error Boundary's render method with conditional logic to display an error message when an error is captured.
4. Wrap the component within the Error Boundary.

<!--
  Your bug will get fixed much faster if we can run your code and it doesn't
  have dependencies other than React. Issues without reproduction steps or
  code examples may be immediately closed as not actionable.
-->

Link to code example: https://codesandbox.io/s/react-18-error-boundary-wrkgry

<!--
  Please provide a CodeSandbox (https://codesandbox.io/s/new), a link to a
  repository on GitHub, or provide a minimal code example that reproduces the
  problem. You may provide a screenshot of the application if you think it is
  relevant to your bug report. Here are some tips for providing a minimal
  example: https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve.
-->

## The current behavior

If a component has code that throws an error **only once** during all the renders, then the Error Boundary will call the static method `getDerivedStateFromError`, update its state, and invoke its render method.

The Error Boundary component will be in a state where it should display an error message instead of `children`. However, React does not update it, despite the state being changed and `render` method being called. Additionally, the `componentDidCatch` method is not called, even though `getDerivedStateFromError` is invoked.

The component that threw the error will **still be displayed.**

**However, I expect React to update the Error Boundary immediately after the first error**

I believe that this contradicts the concept outlined in [the old React documentation](https://legacy.reactjs.org/docs/error-boundaries.html#new-behavior-for-uncaught-errors):

> We debated this decision, but in our experience it is worse to leave corrupted UI in place than to completely remove it.

## The expected behavior

Just as in React 17.0.2, I expect that immediately after the first error thrown in the component, React will not only call the `getDerivedStateFromError` method in the nearest Error Boundary, update its state, and invoke the `render` method, but it will also call the `componentDidCatch` method and apply changes to the screen according to its state: hiding `children` and displaying an error message. 

Otherwise, if React deems that a component, which threw an error  is still displayable on the screen, then why isn't `componentDidCatch` called at the very least? I expect to report the error to my reporting service, but I'm unable to do so in React 18.2.0

title: Bug: onClick doesn't work on any element inside disabled fieldset in Firefox
body:
React version: 18.2.0
Firefox version: Firefox 118.0.2

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Firefox 118 (as well as older versions) and React 18
2. Within React
3. Inside a `fieldset` with `disabled={true}`
4. Add an `a` tag, `span` tag, or presumably any other tag.
5. Add an `onClick` (or `onMouseDown`, `onMouseUp`) handler to the `a` tag.
6. Click the `a` tag

Link to code example:

https://codepen.io/pygmael/pen/KKbEPqb?editors=1010

## The current behavior
The `onClick` handler is not run.

## The expected behavior
The `onClick` handler gets run.

## Notes

### In React:
- Same issue occurs with other "clicky" events: `onMouseDown`, `onMouseUp`
- Does not happen with `onMouseOver`, etc.
- Does not happen if you add the handler directly to the element via `addEventListener`
- Chrome does not exhibit this issue.

### Outside React:
- Issue does not occur with `onclick` or `addEventListener`
- Issue **does** occur when "delegating" the handler by adding it to document and filtering based on the selector. This is presumably what ReactDOM is doing.

This is arguably a Firefox bug (click events on non-form elements within a disabled fieldset don't make it out of the fieldset.) Ideally though, React's `onClick` behavior would match `onclick` or `addEventListener` in the browsers it supports, rather than making the user understand React's implementation (which I don't think is documented.)

There are a couple of related bugzilla issues ([1][bugzilla1], [2][bugzilla2]), though neither appears to quite hit the nail on the head.

[bugzilla1]: https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1200127
[bugzilla2]: https://bugzilla.mozilla.org/show_bug.cgi?id=1637321

title: Improvement: Add a log when react-dom is running on RSC instead of SSR
body:
<!--
  Please provide a clear and concise description of what the bug is. Include
  screenshots if needed. Please test using the latest version of the relevant
  React packages to make sure your issue has not already been fixed.
-->
When a SSR file is being wrongly considered as an RSC, the error log is not easy to understand. After #27436 react-dom started importing only the react.shared-subset file and not the react.production. This causes the error [here](https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/dddfe688206dafa5646550d351eb9a8e9c53654a/packages/react-server/src/ReactFizzServer.js#L3504) where `ReactCurrentCache` is undefined. React version: `canary`, `>= 18.3.0-canary-a6ed60a8e-20230929`

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Wrongly consider an SSR file as part of RSC (react-server condition)

title: Bug: On mobile devices, clicking a checkbox in a list of checkboxes will sometimes toggle the wrong one.
body:
When rendering a list of checkboxes, clicking checkboxes in sequence will sometimes trigger the wrong checkbox to be toggled. Behavior seems to be that sometimes previously clicked checkbox gets toggled instead of currently clicked checkbox.

* This issue only seems to be on mobile or possibly touch devices. Could possibly be an issue with touch event listener.
* This issue seems to have been introduced in React 17.0.0 as version 16.14.0 seems to work correctly.

Specs:
React version: >=17.0.0 (18.2.0)
device: iPhone 8 plus
browser: Chrome version 117.0.5938.117

## Steps To Reproduce

1. View app on a mobile device (I'm using iPhone 8 plus with Chrome)
2. Click multiple checkboxes in sequence
3. Expect that correct checkbox always gets toggled, however sometimes previous checkbox gets toggled.

Link to code example:

Sandbox with working behavior (React 16.14.0)
https://codesandbox.io/s/react-16-checkboxes-xzzl96
https://xzzl96.csb.app/

Sandbox with broken behavior (React 18.2.0)
https://codesandbox.io/s/react-18-checkboxes-zrhpfp
https://zrhpfp.csb.app/

## The current behavior
When clicking multiple checkboxes in sequence, sometimes the wrong checkboxes gets toggled.

## The expected behavior
The correct checkbox should be toggled when clicked.

title: Bug: react-error-boundary using class fields unsupported in Safari 13.1
body:
We are using webpack to bundle a React app that includes react error boundaries.
When running our app in Safari 13.1, the app fails to render due to syntax errors in react-error-boundary due to its use of public class fields, which are not supported.

React version: 17.0.1

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Bundle app with react error boundaries
2. Open app in Safari 13.1

## The current behavior
Browser fails to parse script and outputs "error  Parsing error: Unexpected token ="

## The expected behavior
I would expect React features to work in ES5 supported browsers. If this is an intentional usage of modern JS, I would have expected it to be documented here: https://legacy.reactjs.org/docs/javascript-environment-requirements.html
author: hoorncj-student

title: Bug: Intersection Observer/reconciliation is incorrect when array changes order
body:
Rendering components based off an array and then reordering the array causes the Intersection Observer to improperly register which elements are currently in the viewport.

I'm submitting this here because I'm assuming that the Intersection Observer implementation is correct. Our guess is that it has something to do with reacts reconciliation.

React version: 18.2

## Steps To Reproduce

1. In the code sandbox linked below, scroll down so that boxes four and (at least partially) five are in the viewport.
2. Click **REORDER** at the top left of the page. This will cause a swap of boxes four and eight, so now eight is in the viewport and four is not.
3. You will notice that box five is now goldenrod, meaning that it is not considered to be in the viewport (a class is added to change the background-color).
4. You can scroll further so that box four is at the top of the page and upon clicking "REORDER" you'll see that it is in fact every box _after_ box four that is now goldenrod, again indicating that it is not in the viewport.


Link to code example: [Code Sandbox](https://codesandbox.io/s/divine-firefly-w5vqm8?file=/src/box.js:331-347)

## The current behavior
Elements that are in the viewport after swapping their position in an array from which they were rendered are considered to _not_ be in the viewport.

## The expected behavior
Elements that are in the viewport should be registered as such.
author: bradevans

title: Bug: StrictMode is not detecting "ref-trashing"
body:
React version:
- any (18.2.0 currently)

## Steps To Reproduce

1. create unstable ref on a component (`<div ref={(node) => setState(node)}/>`) 
2. render/re-render component 
3. StrictMode will not report about the problem of ref being unstable between renders

Link to code example:

there are many different ways to create unstable ref:
- use `createRef` instead of `useRef` (happens)
- use functions without `useCallback`
- use different "mergeRefs" solutions leading to unstable refs, see https://github.com/gregberge/react-merge-refs/issues/5

## The current behavior

- nothing enforces the correct usage of refs or able to inform about the incorrect ones


## The expected behavior

- StrictMode is capable of reporting refs being updated on every render

-----

I've monkey-patched react code (`markRef` [here](https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/34de2986dfeddda31cb76d298c6d59b271d976fa/packages/react-reconciler/src/ReactFiberBeginWork.js#L1087) and [here](https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/34de2986dfeddda31cb76d298c6d59b271d976fa/packages/react-reconciler/src/ReactFiberCompleteWork.js#L1227)) to get informed about ref change and found a few changes that should not happen. 

This a problem very hard to detect without a support from the platform, and my little experiment is incapable to understand the legacy of ref update, only the fact of such update.
author: theKashey

title: Bug: eslint-plugin-react-hooks rules-of-hooks misses hook usage in class properties with typescript-eslint v5+ parser
body:
React version: any version with hooks
`eslint-plugin-react-hooks` version: 4.6.0 / all (still repros in 5.0.0-canary-f81c0f1ed-20230927)

## Steps To Reproduce

Link to code example: https://github.com/ecraig12345/repro-react-hooks-eslint

1. Install dependencies:
   - `eslint-plugin-react-hooks` (any version)
   - `@typescript-eslint/parser` v5 or greater 
   - `eslint` v6 or greater
   - `typescript` (any version)
2. In the project's eslint config, set the parser to `@typescript-eslint/parser` and enable the `react-hooks/rules-of-hooks` rule
```
{
  parser: '@typescript-eslint/parser',
  plugins: ['react-hooks'],
  rules: {
    'react-hooks/rules-of-hooks': 'error',
  }
}
```
3. Add some code that calls a hook inside a class *property* function (*not* a class method)
```ts
import { useState } from 'react';
class Test {
  useHook = () => {
    useState();
  }
}
```
4. Run eslint on the project

## The current behavior

No errors are flagged.

## The expected behavior

One error is flagged.

The behavior was correct with `@typescript-eslint/parser` v4 (as demonstrated in the [full repro](https://github.com/ecraig12345/repro-react-hooks-eslint)).

## Root cause

In [`@typescript-eslint/parser` v5](https://github.com/typescript-eslint/typescript-eslint/releases/tag/v5.0.0), the AST node type `ClassProperty` was renamed to `PropertyDefinition`. The `rules-of-hooks` rule [needs to be updated](https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/34de2986dfeddda31cb76d298c6d59b271d976fa/packages/eslint-plugin-react-hooks/src/RulesOfHooks.js#L521) to check for this additional type.

title: Bug: I can't opt out of Suspense on SSR (using Next.js in dev)
body:
According to [`Suspense` doc](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense):

> If a component throws an error on the server, React will not abort the server render. Instead, it will find the closest <Suspense> component above it and include its fallback (such as a spinner) into the generated server HTML. The user will see a spinner at first.

This works as expected in production mode when I build then run my next app. However, in dev mode, the error overlay is shown, making the strategy not very practical.

React version: 18.2.0

## Steps To Reproduce

Just open this   at lockdown-run.js:4:3
```
and
```
lockdown-run.js:17 Lockdown failed: TypeError: At intrinsics.Object.groupBy expected boolean not function
  at isAllowedPropertyValue (lockdown-install.js:1:53384)
  at isAllowedProperty (lockdown-install.js:1:53807)
  at visitProperties (lockdown-install.js:1:55095)
  at isAllowedPropertyValue (lockdown-install.js:1:53041)
  at isAllowedProperty (lockdown-install.js:1:53807)
  at visitProperties (lockdown-install.js:1:55095)
  at lockdown-install.js:1:55523
  at repairIntrinsics (lockdown-install.js:1:144597)
  at lockdown-install.js:1:145462
  at lockdown-run.js:4:3
```
The website works fine but having errors in the console can impact google SEO and is generally not best practice.

## The expected behavior
No errors in the console, at least building the react app removes the errors and not with a temporary fix such as `console.clear()`.

title: Bug: Suspense does not work in Server Components with `use` api
body:
<!--
  Please provide a clear and concise description of what the bug is. Include
  screenshots if needed. Please test using the latest version of the relevant
  React packages to make sure your issue has not already been fixed.
-->

React version: 18.3.0-canary-dd480ef92-20230822

## Steps To Reproduce

1.  Clone the demo of React Server Components https://github.com/reactjs/server-components-demo
2.  Enable the sleep for NoteList  https://github.com/reactjs/server-components-demo/blob/main/src/NoteList.js#L24

```js
export default async function NoteList({searchText}) {
  // ...

  // Now let's see how the Suspense boundary above lets us not block on this.
  await fetch('http://localhost:4000/sleep/3000');

  return ...
}
```
<!--
  Your bug will get fixed much faster if we can run your code and it doesn't
  have dependencies other than React. Issues without reproduction steps or
  code examples may be immediately closed as not actionable.
-->

<!--
  Please provide a CodeSandbox (https://codesandbox.io/s/new), a link to a
  repository on GitHub, or provide a minimal code example that reproduces the
  problem. You may provide a screenshot of the application if you think it is
  relevant to your bug report. Here are some tips for providing a minimal
  example: https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve.
-->

## The current behavior

```jsx
<Suspense fallback={<NoteListSkeleton />}>
   <NoteList searchText={searchText} />
</Suspense>
```
Although `NoteList` is wrapped by `Suspense`, the main page does not start rendering until the `NodeList` is ready. Before that, the page has been in a white screen state.

## The expected behavior

The rest of page render first, and the NoteList is rendered streaming.

## Additional information

I guess this is because the demo is use `use` api to get the content of react tree response.

```js
import { createFromFetch } from 'react-server-dom-webpack/client';

export function Router() {
  // ..
  content = createFromFetch(
    fetch('/react?location=' + encodeURIComponent(locationKey))
  );
  // ...

  return (
    <RouterContext.Provider value={{location, navigate, refresh}}>
      {use(content)}
    </RouterContext.Provider>
  );
}
```

`use` will wait until the is response is done and then update the content of  Suspense Component. Therefore, although react tree is returned by streaming, it will not be rendered by streaming.

title: Bug: react-test-renderer types of .toTree API are missing
body:
<!--
  Please provide a clear and concise description of what the bug is. Include
  screenshots if needed. Please test using the latest version of the relevant
  React packages to make sure your issue has not already been fixed.
-->

React version:

## Steps To Reproduce

1. use [`testRenderer.toTree()`](https://legacy.reactjs.org/docs/test-renderer.html#testrenderertotree) API and it's wrong types

<!--
  Your bug will get fixed much faster if we can run your code and it doesn't
  have dependencies other than React. Issues without reproduction steps or
  code examples may be immediately closed as not actionable.
-->

Link to code example:

https://codesandbox.io/s/suspicious-edison-swhsg3?file=/src/children-primitives.tsx

<!--
  Please provide a CodeSandbox (https://codesandbox.io/s/new), a link to a
  repository on GitHub, or provide a minimal code example that reproduces the
  problem. You may provide a screenshot of the application if you think it is
  relevant to your bug report. Here are some tips for providing a minimal
  example: https://stackoverflow.com/help/mcve.
-->

## The current behavior

Currently types of [`testRenderer.toTree()`](https://legacy.reactjs.org/docs/test-renderer.html#testrenderertotree) are missing.

1. `children` may be of any primitive (not just `string`)
2. `children` must not only be `null` or an array, but may also be a single node object

**Note:** types of [`testRenderer.toTree()`](https://legacy.reactjs.org/docs/test-renderer.html#testrenderertotree) are even committed as **$FlowFixMe**
https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/2807d781a08db8e9873687fccc25c0f12b4fb3d4/packages/react-test-renderer/src/ReactTestRenderer.js#L179

Instead those types are provided:
https://github.com/facebook/react/blob/2807d781a08db8e9873687fccc25c0f12b4fb3d4/packages/react-test-renderer/src/ReactTestRenderer.js#L69-L75

## The expected behavior

[`testRenderer.toTree()`](https://legacy.reactjs.org/docs/test-renderer.html#testrenderertotree) should be properly typed.

## Related

https://github.com/DefinitelyTyped/DefinitelyTyped/discussions/65263
author: AndyOGo

title: Bug: react-dom/server.bun in `@canary` hangs without flushing or terminating the connection on long class names(?)
body:
React version: canary ## Steps To Reproduce See https://github.com/oven-sh/bun/issues/5664 1. Run `bun ./server.tsx`: ```tsx import { type Server as BunServer } from "bun"; import { renderToReadableStream } from "react-dom/server"; Bun.serve({ hostname: "0.0.0.0", port: 3000, fetch: async (request: Request, server: BunServer) => { return new Response( await renderToReadableStream(
), { headers: { "Content-Type": "text/html", }, } ); }, }); ``` 2. In another terminal, run `oha http://localhost:3000` (`oha` can be installed with `cargo install oha`, or you can use bombardier, wrk, or most other HTTP benchmarking tools) This started sometime in the last few days on a recent `@canary` build of react-dom/server. ## The current behavior The connections never close until timeout, which eventually leads to resource exhaustion ``` [2596] Can't assign requested address (os error 49) ``` This does not happen when the request is suffiiciently small. It doesn't reproduce, for example, on Bun's homepage benchmark. It happens in a larger request/response. My current guess is there is some chunking/batching logic in react-dom/server that needs to flush and it is not flushing in the bun version. ## The expected behavior It should work. I tried going back quite far in Bun versions and the behavior is the same. I suspect this is caused by a recent change in react-dom. But I'm not sure. It's also entirely possible that this is a bug in Bun. But I wanted to make sure to file an issue so your team knows before 18.3 is released non-canary.

title: [Feature Request]: RSC error - allow error.code or an other specialized prop to be forwarded to the client
body:
I've observed that React Server Components currently send a digest to the client in case of an error, without forwarding the exact error. This behavior can sometimes be limiting, especially when we want to handle specific error cases on the client-side, like the 401 Unauthorized status.

To tackle this, I've introduced a mechanism that selectively forwards specific error codes to the client, without compromising on security or exposing sensitive error messages.

### Possible solutions:

1. **Forwarding the error.code Property:**

* Altered the server component's response handler to recognize and extract the `error.code` property during error scenarios.
* If the `error.code` is present and matches our predefined whitelist, it's forwarded to the client. This allows the client to understand specific error types without exposing detailed server messages.

2. **Custom Digest Configuration:**

* Implemented a mechanism for developers to provide a custom digest. 
* The default behavior remains to send the generic digest unless the developer specifies this custom function, providing flexibility without compromising security.

3. **Introduction of a Specific Error Prop:**

* Introduced a new property to the error object, named `clientHint`. This prop can be set on the server-side and is designed to provide hints or specific behaviors that the client should adopt upon encountering the error.
* On the client-side, React components can now access this `clientHint` prop and act accordingly, giving developers a structured way to pass actionable instructions from server to client during error scenarios.

### Benefits of this Change:

* Efficient Error Handling: Allows client-side code to react immediately based on the error code, without making an additional request.
* Enhanced User Experience: Enables smoother user redirects or prompts based on specific error scenarios.
* Developer Flexibility: By making the whitelist configurable, we provide developers control over error handling without compromising on security.
Looking Forward:

Looking forward to the community's thoughts!

Thank you!

title: Control blocking vs lazy behavior of Suspense during renderToPipeableStream
body:
When using renderToPipeableStream, it would be useful if the renderer could block on certain Suspense boundaries instead of using the fallback and streamed rendering. For example, our app shell consists of static data (script/CSS tags, etc.) as well as some loading state HTML. The loading state HTML unfortunately still depends on an async network request made outside of React. It would be convenient to structure our app like this:

```jsx
<html>
  <head>
    <script src="..." />
    <link href ="..." />
    {/* various script, link, meta tags */}
  </head>
  <body>
    <Suspense>
      {/* this suspends with initialStatePromise internally */}
      <AppShell initialState={initialStatePromise}>
         {/* Our actual app, with its own internal suspense boundaries */}
         <App />
      </AppShell>
    </Suspense>
  </body>
</html
```

This would ensure that:

1. Static assets are flushed immediately
2. The AppShell suspends on the initial state and renders when that's ready
3. Our actual application code can fetch data and render product-specific loading states normally, with HTML streaming

For various technical (or ideological...) reasons, we want that the initial loading state to be visible without depending on React's JS to patch things up. As a result, we can't have that Suspense boundary around the AppShell - React would suspend and send an empty `<body>`. We need to await the `initialStatePromise` before we begin rendering. This in turn delays our static script/link tags, hurting our time to paint. 

We could solve this if we could tell React to *block* on `initialStatePromise`, instead of rendering the fallback and moving on. I have a couple of ideas for what this API could look like, as well as ways to implement this outside of React.

### Userland implementations

I read https://github.com/reactwg/react-18/discussions/106 and https://github.com/reactwg/react-18/discussions/108 and the general idea of transforming React's response stream seems applicable here. We could do something super hacky like wrapping React's stream and inspecting each chunk to perform the stitching that React's runtime would've done client-side. Upon encountering a blocking Suspense boundary, the transform stream would start buffering React's output, while waiting for the rendered content to appear. 

Another approach could be to simply handle the app shell ourselves (by writing directly to the response); this is what we were doing pre-react 18 anyway. However, the docs for `renderToPipeableStream` [explicitly state](https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/server/renderToPipeableStream#parameters):
> It is expected to represent the entire document, so the App component should render the <html> tag

### Possible React API

renderToPipeableStream could take a list of `blockingPromises`. If caught by a Suspense boundary, these promises would pause the renderer and response stream . I'm not sure how feasible this is with the renderer internals, though - it'd have to stop at a half-open tag and partially rendered component.

author: mshmyw

title: Bug: Hydration fails in Next.js with server actions actions custom tags
body:
I'm currently testing the experimental server actions feature in Next.js. Once this feature is enabled Next.js uses React's experimental build. This however results in hydration failed error messages when using custom HTML tags with a dash like `my-tag` in JSX. The server response does not include any output for this tag and its children. Replacing the custom tag with a regular tag like `<div>` works completely fine.

I previously reported this issue at vercel/next.js#51141 where they suspect this to be an issue within React itself.

React version: Experimental

## Steps To Reproduce

1. Install latest next.js: npx create-[next-app](https://linear.app/vercel/issue/NEXT-app)@latest
2. Enable server actions in next.config.js
3. Wrap {children} in root layout with custom HTML tag like `<my-tag>`

Link to code example:
https://github.com/mritzerfeld/next-server-action-with-custom-tag

## The current behavior
Server-generated contents with custom HTML tags do no produce any output for such.

## The expected behavior
Server-generated contents within custom HTML tags to be included in server response, as using Next without server actions or with regular HTML tags.

title: Natneam Mesele wants to pay 0 USD to have this issue fixed
body:
Description:
I've encountered an issue with the React Developer Tools extension where it seems to be injecting or interacting with the renderer.js file in a non-React web page context when the "React Developer Tools" extension is installed in Chrome and the Developer Tools (F12) are open. This is leading to unexpected behavior and possible errors. I would like to report this issue for further investigation.

Steps to Reproduce:
Open a non-React web page in Chrome.
Install and activate the "React Developer Tools" extension from the Chrome Web Store.
Open the Developer Tools (F12) in Chrome.
Observe the presence of the renderer.js file in the sources or network tab.
Refresh the page.
Notice any unusual behavior or errors occurring on the page.
Expected Behavior:

The React Developer Tools extension should only interact with React applications and components. It shouldn't affect or modify the behavior of non-React web pages.

Actual Behavior:
The extension appears to be interacting with the renderer.js file in non-React web pages when the "React Developer Tools" extension is installed and Chrome's Developer Tools are open, leading to unexpected behavior and possibly causing errors.

Additional Information:
Browser: Google Chrome
React Developer Tools Extension Version: 4.28.0
Operating System: window10
Please let me know if there's any additional information I can provide to help investigate and resolve this issue.
Thank you for your attention to this matter. 
 For more details visit [https://mintycode.io](https://mintycode.io/profile?action=CREATOR_SUPPORT_MODAL&requestId=4a01648c-dda9-4d93-a729-617d7065d0c3&owner=facebook&name=react)

title: Bug: script inside script render with SSR + renderToPipeableStream
body:
The question/bug is about SSR + renderToPipeableStream

There is an idea, how to Inject something Into the SSR Stream in [reactwg/react-18](https://github.com/reactwg/react-18/discussions/114) by @sebmarkbage And yes, works perfectly. For example, I use it to inject to an html data, that were fetched on the server.

There can be situation, when `<script>` inside another `<script>` returns from renderToPipeableStream

React version: 18.2.0

## Steps To Reproduce

Link to code example:

There is the source code: https://github.com/artem-malko/react-ssr-template
There is my injector: https://github.com/artem-malko/react-ssr-template/blob/main/src/framework/applications/server/utils/reactStreamRenderEnhancer.ts
And the usage: https://github.com/artem-malko/react-ssr-template/blob/main/src/framework/applications/server/createApplicationRouteHandler.tsx#L317

This project in action: http://174.138.13.187:5000/users?p=1 But it is extremely hard to repeat the bug  

## The current behavior

Sometimes I get a quite interesting situation, when there is a script tag (just `<script>`)  inside chunk. This script contains a config for an application. And I try to add one more script to a page by using this injector pattern. So, I see that script internals (the code) on the client side cause of `<script>` inside another `<script>`. 

## The expected behavior

Actually I don't know, how to fix this problem(

I can wrap the whole app with `<Suspense>` (that part, which is not in a shell. I mean https://github.com/artem-malko/react-ssr-template/blob/main/src/framework/applications/shell.tsx#L57C9-L57C19). But I'm not sure it is a good idea

title: Bug: useEffect not firing when depending on hook value
body:
React version: 18.2.0 ## Steps To Reproduce 1. Setup a hook which returns a `ref.current` value. 2. Setup a `useEffect` which depends on hooks return value. 3. Have some `useState` which causes re-renders. 4. The `useEffect` may not be called. Link to code example: https://codesandbox.io/s/zealous-jasper-fvynsw?file=/src/App.js Be sure to open the dev console **Relevant code:** ```javascript import React, { useRef, useEffect, useState } from "react"; export default function App() { const hooksValue = useHookThatReturnsRefValue(); console.info("Render:", hooksValue); useEffect(() => { console.info("useEffect ran! ---------------------", hooksValue); }, [hooksValue]); return
View dev console
; } function useHookThatReturnsRefValue() { const [state, setState] = useState(0); const ref = useRef(0); useEffect(() => { setState((old) => (old === 0 ? 1 : old)); }, [state]); useEffect( function loadInitialRange() { if (state === 1) { ref.current += 1; const timeout = setTimeout(() => { setState((old) => (old === 1 ? 0 : old)); }, 2000); return () => clearTimeout(timeout); } }, [state] ); return ref.current; } ``` ## The current behavior The console output looks as follows: ``` index.js:27 Render: 0 index.js:27 useEffect ran! --------------------- 0 index.js:27 Render: 0 index.js:27 Render: 1 index.js:27 Render: 1 index.js:27 Render: 1 index.js:27 Render: 2 index.js:27 Render: 2 index.js:27 Render: 2 index.js:27 Render: 3 index.js:27 Render: 3 index.js:27 Render: 3 index.js:27 Render: 4 index.js:27 Render: 4 index.js:27 Render: 4 index.js:27 Render: 5 ``` The `useEffect` is not called, but its impossible to know why. This is an important issue, as there are no warnings about returning values from a hook that cannot be used in dependency arrays. ## The expected behavior I would expect the console output to look as follows: ``` index.js:27 Render: 0 index.js:27 useEffect ran! --------------------- 0 index.js:27 Render: 1 index.js:27 useEffect ran! --------------------- 1 index.js:27 Render: 2 index.js:27 useEffect ran! --------------------- 2 index.js:27 Render: 2 index.js:27 Render: 3 index.js:27 useEffect ran! --------------------- 3 index.js:27 Render: 4 index.js:27 useEffect ran! --------------------- 4 index.js:27 Render: 4 index.js:27 Render: 5 index.js:27 useEffect ran! --------------------- 5 ```

author: erjoalgo

title: Bug: some /public resources not served when proxy is enabled in manifest.
body:
I'm using react within a Docker container with an accompanying backend. In order to minimize the number of ports exposed, I am attempting to have react proxy requests internally to the backend within the internal docker container network, as opposed to having the browser talk directly to the backend.

So I set the "proxy" key in `package.json`:

```
...
"proxy": "http://service:3000",
...
```

Requests to the backend are served correctly. Also, /public/index.html is served correctly.

However, other resources under the react public directory, e.g. `/public/manifest.json`, `/public/favicon.ico` are also proxied to the backend, which in my case results in a 404.

Though it's not explicitly stated, from the documentation I get the impression that only `unknown` requests are proxied, but I would expect the static resources under /public mentioned above should be recognized and served instead of proxied.

```
To tell the development server to proxy any unknown requests to your API server in development, add a proxy field to your package.json, for example:

  "proxy": "http://localhost:4000",
This way, when you fetch('/api/todos') in development, the development server will recognize that it’s not a static asset, and will proxy your request to http://localhost:4000/api/todos as a fallback. The development server will only attempt to send requests without text/html in its Accept header to the proxy.

Conveniently, this avoids [CORS issues](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/21854516/understanding-ajax-cors-and-security-considerations) and error messages like this in development:
```

React version:  "react": "^18.0.0",

## Steps To Reproduce

1. set the "proxy" property and start react
2. attempt to fetch a non-text/html resource under /public

Link to code example:


## The current behavior
/public/manifest.json is proxied to backend, resulting in 404


## The expected behavior
/public/manifest.json and other static files are recognized as a known resource and served
author: erjoalgo

title: [React: re-render]: On changing search value: it re-render the whole component
body:
React version: 18.2.0 ## Steps To Reproduce 1. I want to add the search functionality based on the search text. I have used a separate component for the search field and then using the props I am setting the value of the state (SearchText). I am adding the full code here for both files. ProjectList component: ``` export default function ProjectDetailPanelContent() { const [hoveredRow, setHoveredRow] = useState(null); const [searchText, setSearchText] = useState(''); const handleSearchTextChange = useCallback( (newSearchText: string) => { setSearchText(newSearchText); console.log('search text', searchText); }, [searchText] ); console.log('project list re-render'); // eslint-disable-next-line react-hooks/exhaustive-deps const projects = [ { id: 1, organization: 'Adaptis', name: 'Bridgeland Place', location: '736 McDougall Court NE, Calgary', canadianClimateZone: '2', clientName: 'Client Name', currency: 'CAD', unit: 'SI', leaders: ['user1'], viewers: ['user2'], disabled: false, }, { id: 2, organization: 'Adaptis', name: 'Bridgeland', location: '325 Court NE, Toronto', canadianClimateZone: '2', clientName: 'Client Name', currency: 'USD', unit: 'IMPERIAL', leaders: ['user1'], viewers: ['user3'], disabled: false, }, ]; // Filter the projects array based on the searchText const filteredProjects = useMemo(() => { if (!searchText) { return projects; } return projects.filter((project) => project.name.toLowerCase().includes(searchText.toLowerCase())); }, [projects, searchText]); const columns: GridColDef[] = useMemo( () => [ { field: 'name', headerName: 'Project', flex: 2, }, { field: 'location', headerName: 'Project Address', flex: 2, }, { field: 'actions', headerName: '', renderCell: (params: GridCellParams) => ( ), sortable: false, filterable: false, disableColumnMenu: true, flex: 1, }, { field: 'action', headerName: '', renderCell: (params: GridCellParams) => , sortable: false, filterable: false, flex: 1, cellClassName: 'cell-content-display-end', }, ], [hoveredRow] ); const handleRowHover = (id: number | null) => { setHoveredRow(id); }; const getDetailPanelHeight = useCallback(() => 'auto', []); return ( , }} pagination initialState={{ pagination: { paginationModel: { pageSize: 10 } }, }} pageSizeOptions={[5, 10]} rows={filteredProjects} columns={columns} onRowHover={handleRowHover} getDetailPanelHeight={getDetailPanelHeight} getDetailPanelContent={(params: GridRowParams) => { return ; }} > ); } ``` and separate component for the Search box, ``` const CustomToolbar = ({ onSearch }: Props) => { const [localSearchText, setLocalSearchText] = useState(''); const debouncedSearch = useMemo(() => debounce(onSearch, 400), [onSearch]); console.log('custom toolbar re-render'); const handleChange = (e: ChangeEvent) => { const newSearchText = e.target.value; setLocalSearchText(newSearchText); debouncedSearch(newSearchText); }; return (
), }} />
); }; export default CustomToolbar; ``` ## The current behaviour I am facing a weird issue with the search box. when start typing I can only type one character, after that, it will re-render the whole component and again I have to focus on the search box to type. Again, I can type only one character. The main thing is changing the search value, it re-renders the whole component again and again. ## The expected behavior I wnat smooth search experience. Please help me to fix this bug.

title: React Way to Make Immediate Changes on DOM
body:
Hi everyone! For some situations, I need to run a script that modifies DOM, for example a script to load images or show a banner on the top of the screen. I need to do those but cannot do it inside useEffect since it lasts too late. Doing it with a script that is used with dangerouslyınnerHtml can load it immidately but causes to rerender after useEffect lasts. Is there a way to load things like those immidately like dangerouslyınnerHtml, without waiting useEffect lasts. Any best practices or different ways?

Thank you!

title: Feature request: TODO task
body:
## Issue: Implement useTransition hook for async strategy in handleChange function

### File
fixtures/concurrent/time-slicing/src/index.js

### Problem:
The current codebase contains a TODO in the `handleChange` function where it suggests using the `useTransition` hook instead of directly calling `setState` for the 'async' strategy. This enhancement aims to optimize the rendering behavior by leveraging the benefits of `useTransition`.

### Proposed Solution:
Replace the existing `this.setState({value})` with a `startTransition` block that wraps the `this.setState({value})` call. This way, the asynchronous state update will be managed by the `useTransition` hook, improving rendering performance in 'async' strategy cases

### Concerns
Since hooks cannot be used in class components my proposed solution is to refactor the App component that is in the code to a functional component.

### Steps to Implement:
1. Replace the existing `this.setState({value})` with a `startTransition` block.
2. Test the functionality thoroughly to ensure the behavior is consistent with the other strategies.

### Benefits:
- Improves rendering performance by using `useTransition` for asynchronous state updates.
- Enhances codebase by adhering to best practices and leveraging React's latest features.
author: pcha07

title: Bug: react.dev tutorial-tic-tac-toe sample project errors out in modern node versions
body:
Following the step by step instructions for tutorial-tic-tac-toe on react.dev results in errors that make the tutorial impossible to continue.

React version: `"react": "^18.0.0",` (as specified in package.json)
Node version: 20.5.1

## Steps To Reproduce

Follow the instructions for using your local development environment at https://react.dev/learn/tutorial-tic-tac-toe#setup-for-the-tutorial and proceed through the five steps:

> 1. Install [Node.js](https://nodejs.org/en/)
> 2. In the CodeSandbox tab you opened earlier, press the top-left corner button to open the menu, and then choose File > Export to ZIP in that menu to download an archive of the files locally
> 3. Unzip the archive, then open a terminal and cd to the directory you unzipped
> 4. Install the dependencies with npm install
> 5. Run npm start to start a local server and follow the prompts to view the code running in a browser

When running `npm start`, see errors in the console, and in the browser:

![image](https://github.com/facebook/react/assets/139312/59adb6a0-4e67-4bf9-8a81-6b429c3b491b)

![image](https://github.com/facebook/react/assets/139312/92c630c4-d992-4a11-bf98-1a04998fbada)

**The process works as intended when using node versions 15 or lower, but fails in the way described above in 16 and above, including the latest, v20**

If the intention is that the tutorial files should be run in an outdated version of node, that should be specified up front in the tutorial docs, and enforced in the source code files provided, e.g.

```
"engines" : { 
    "node" : "<=15"
  }
```

## The current behavior

The application errors out instead of running properly, with no useful error message for the user indicating that this is a problem with the node version.

## The expected behavior

The application should _not_ error out, it should run properly, or else should properly notify the user that their node version is incorrect for this codebase.

title: Bug: moving item in list resets scroll when item has `key` prop
body: React version: 18.2.0 ## Steps To Reproduce 1. In the example, scroll a little bit (make sure "item 6" is still in the view). 2. Click the button to move "item 6" to top, notice scroll position reset to top | before: | after moving item 6: | | ---- |-----| ||| Link to code example: https://codesandbox.io/s/great-haibt-2ztxmq?file=/src/App.js ## The current behavior Moving "item 6" to top resets the scroll position. However, if I scroll to the very bottom and "item 6" is not in the visible area, moving "item 6" doesn't reset scroll. It seems like an inconsistency in behavior. title: Bug: Safari gets stuck when listening to "wheel" event and element size changes
body: React version: 18 ## Steps To Reproduce Actually it's not a React bug, but a Safari one. I just reported it to them: https://bugs.webkit.org/show_bug.cgi?id=260253 Briefly, If you are listening to the "wheel" event, changing the size of the element will take extremely long, even if the listener doesn't do anything. The problem is that React automatically adds a listener to the "wheel" event when creating the root element, and there seems to be no way to disable it from users side. Repro steps: 1. Open this [html](https://github.com/turkey1zh/Safari-Bug-Repro/blob/ff09658fa3e626b3be7bb654d6ed8c0d1df8e7d3/test-react.html) by using Safari 2. Change Safari window width to the minimum size 3. Open the Web Inspector 4. Enable/disable the "margin-left" attribute of the root element (You'll notice the rendering took a long time) ## The current behavior When enabling "margin-left", Safari gets stuck for a while. ## The expected behavior Safari won't get stuck. I'm not sure how long it will take Safari to fix this. Is it possible to remove this event from React side or give some options to disable it?

title: Bug: support `fetchPriority` in camel-case on DOM nodes.
body:
At the moment, the HTML fetchPriority is only supported written as `fetchpriority`, see https://github.com/facebook/react/issues/26810

But as per https://github.com/DefinitelyTyped/DefinitelyTyped/pull/65972 it is required in `@types/react` to be written as `fetchPriority` as it is also for other props e.g. `imageSrcSet`. 

With spelling it as camel-case, react ejects a warning:
```
Warning: React does not recognize the `fetchPriority` prop on a DOM element. If you intentionally want it to appear in the DOM as a custom attribute, spell it as lowercase `fetchpriority` instead. If you accidentally passed it from a parent component, remove it from the DOM element.
    at link
    at head
    at Head (webpack-internal:///./node_modules/next/dist/pages/_document.js:258:1)
    at html
    at Html (webpack-internal:///./node_modules/next/dist/pages/_document.js:680:119)
    at MyDocument (webpack-internal:///./pages/_document.tsx:24:1)
```

Relevant versions:
```
"@types/react": "^18.2.20",
"react": "^18.2.0",
"react-dom": "^18.2.0",
"next": "13.4.12",
"typescript": "^5.1.6", 
```

## Steps To Reproduce
Example component: 
```
import React from "react";
import NextHead from "next/head";

type Props = {
    webP: string;
};

export const ImagePreload: React.FC<Props> = React.memo(({ webP }) => (
    <NextHead>
        <link
            key={`image-preload-${webP}`}
            rel="preload"
            as="image"
            imageSrcSet={webP}
            fetchPriority="high"
        />
    </NextHead>
));
ImagePreload.displayName = "ImagePreload";
```

author: Asare6

title: Can createElement be optimized for performance?
body: For large complex apps that have many props and a deep react DOM tree, createElement is one of the most expensive operations. I've noticed for large complex apps such as Facebook and Discord, createElement can take up 10% of the memory allocation during a basic scenario, and it can take up 2-5% of the script time, not accounting for MajorGC and MinorGC. The issue is that the props object in createElement gets created from scratch, and the entire config that's passed in gets iterated over. Since the config object isn't used anymore after createElement, it gets marked for GC, which additionally hurts performance. I have an idea where we can just simply assign props to config for most cases, such as when the props object is not frozen, and no key or ref was passed in to createElement. I can create a PR, but I wanted to run it by the react devs first to see if there is anything I'm missing. Facebook Discord
